question_text: A galvanometer is converted into an ammeter by connecting a:
explanation: An ammeter must have very low resistance so it does not alter the current in the circuit. A low resistance (shunt) connected in parallel with the galvanometer bypasses most of the current, protecting the galvanometer while allowing measurement of large currents.
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: High resistance in series is used for voltmeter conversion, not ammeter.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing ammeter and voltmeter conversion methods

elimination_hints.hint: High resistance in parallel would not bypass sufficient current.
elimination_hints.misconception: Misunderstanding the purpose of shunt resistance

elimination_hints.hint: Low resistance in series would still leave the galvanometer in the main path without protection.

options.text: Resistance equal to the circuit resistance

explanation: An ideal voltmeter should draw no current from the circuit so that it does not disturb the potential difference it is measuring. This requires infinite resistance.
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: Zero resistance describes an ideal ammeter, not a voltmeter.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing ammeter and voltmeter ideal properties

elimination_hints.hint: Low resistance would cause the voltmeter to draw significant current, disturbing the circuit.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not understanding why voltmeters need high resistance

elimination_hints.hint: Matching circuit resistance would still draw appreciable current.

question_text: The resistance of an ideal ammeter is:
explanation: An ideal ammeter should not introduce any additional resistance in the circuit, so its resistance must be zero. This ensures the current being measured remains unchanged.
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: Infinite resistance describes an ideal voltmeter.
High resistance in an ammeter would reduce the circuit current significantly.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not understanding ammeter placement in series

elimination_hints.hint: Matching external resistance would halve the current.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying maximum power transfer theorem inappropriately

question_text: The sensitivity of a moving coil galvanometer can be increased by:

options.text: Increasing the restoring torque per unit twist
Decreasing the number of turns of the coil
Decreasing the area of the coil

explanation: Current sensitivity = NAB/k. Increasing B (magnetic field strength) increases the deflection per unit current, thus increasing sensitivity. Increasing restoring torque (k) or decreasing N or A would decrease sensitivity.
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: Increasing restoring torque k decreases sensitivity since sensitivity = NAB/k.
Decreasing N reduces the deflecting torque and thus sensitivity.
Decreasing area A reduces the torque on the coil.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking smaller coils are more sensitive

question_text: A galvanometer of resistance 50 Ω gives full-scale deflection for a current of 5 mA. To convert it into an ammeter of range 5 A, the shunt resistance required is:
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: Check your calculation — this value is 10 times too large.
A 5 Ω shunt would not bypass enough current for a 5 A range.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not applying the shunt formula correctly

elimination_hints.hint: Equal resistance to the galvanometer would only double the range, not increase it 1000-fold.

question_text: A galvanometer has a resistance of 100 Ω and gives full-scale deflection for 10 mA. To convert it into a voltmeter of range 100 V, the resistance to be connected in series is:
explanation: R = V/Ig - G = 100/(10 × 10⁻³) - 100 = 10000 - 100 = 9900 Ω. A high resistance in series limits the current through the galvanometer to its full-scale deflection value when the full voltage is applied.
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: This is identical to D — but check if one option is distinguished differently in the original.
10000 Ω is V/Ig but forgets to subtract galvanometer resistance G.
Same numerical value as D with different formatting — verify the exact answer.

question_text: A galvanometer has a resistance of 100 Ω and gives full-scale deflection for 10 mA. To convert it into a voltmeter of range 100 V, the resistance to be connected in series is:
explanation: R = V/Ig - G = 100/(10 × 10⁻³) - 100 = 10000 - 100 = 9900 Ω. A high resistance in series limits the current through the galvanometer to its full-scale deflection value when the full voltage is applied.
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: 10,000 Ω is V/Ig but forgets to subtract galvanometer resistance G.
990 Ω is too small — recheck the division V/Ig.
100 Ω is just the galvanometer resistance itself.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the series resistance with galvanometer resistance
